fields.name: Marie Curie
fields.bio_short: Polish-born physicist and chemist who conducted pioneering research on radioactivity and was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize.
fields.bio_long: Marie Sklodowska Curie was a Polish-born physicist and chemist who lived from 1867 to 1934. She conducted groundbreaking research on radioactivity, a term she coined, and discovered two new elements: polonium and radium. She was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize (Physics, 1903) and remains the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different scientific fields (Physics and Chemistry, 1911). During World War I, she developed mobile X-ray units to treat wounded soldiers. Her dedication to science and education, despite societal barriers, established her as a pioneer for women in STEM fields.

fields.name: Nelson Mandela
fields.bio_short: South African anti-apartheid revolutionary and political leader who served as President of South Africa from 1994 to 1999.
fields.bio_long: Nelson Rolihlahla Mandela was born on July 18, 1918, in Mvezo, South Africa, and died on December 5, 2013. He was a South African anti-apartheid revolutionary, political leader, and philanthropist who served as President of South Africa from 1994 to 1999. Mandela was the first black head of state of South Africa and the first president elected in a fully representative democratic election. His government focused on dismantling the legacy of apartheid by tackling institutionalized racism and fostering racial reconciliation. Internationally, Mandela was a respected statesman and advocate for human rights. He received over 250 honors, including the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993. Before his presidency, he was a lawyer, activist, and leader of the African National Congress Youth League. He was imprisoned for 27 years for his anti-apartheid activities.

score_json.summary: A transformative leader who demonstrated extraordinary moral courage in fighting apartheid, forgiving oppressors, and building a unified democratic South Africa. His strategic brilliance, ethical consistency, and universal appeal established him as one of history's greatest reconcilers.

fields.name: Benjamin Franklin
fields.bio_short: American polymath and founding father who contributed to science, politics, diplomacy, and invention.
fields.bio_long: Benjamin Franklin was born on January 17, 1706, in Boston, Massachusetts Bay Colony, and died on April 17, 1790, in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. He was one of the Founding Fathers of the United States and a leading polymath of his time. Franklin was a leading writer, printer, political philosopher, politician, Freemason, postmaster, scientist, inventor, civic activist, statesman, and diplomat. As a scientist, he was a major figure in the American Enlightenment and the history of physics for his discoveries and theories regarding electricity. He invented the lightning rod, bifocals, the Franklin stove, a carriage odometer, and the glass armonica. He facilitated many civic organizations, including Philadelphia's fire department and the University of Pennsylvania. As an inventor and businessman, Franklin became wealthy from his newspaper, printing business, and inventions. He played a major role in establishing the public library, hospital, and college in Philadelphia. He was actively involved in the colonial and early American politics, having achieved great success in French diplomacy during the Revolutionary War. Franklin became a national hero in America as an agent of American patriotism and virtue. His colorful life and versatile career make him one of the most admired figures in American history.

score_json.summary: A quintessential Renaissance man who excelled across intellectual, civic, and practical domains. His innovative spirit, strategic mind, and commitment to public service helped establish foundational American institutions and values. His ability to combine theoretical insight with practical application exemplified the Enlightenment ideal.
